speaker: interviewer
text: We are interviewing Max Rosenbloom. And we're tape four. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was [? still ?] living in the hospital, the doctors came in. And they told me what they want to do. And they gave me papers to sign. So I said, let me think over it over a day. And that time, I didn't know for nobody there. I knew that everybody is gone. I have got no family, no nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So next day, they came. And they wanted back the paper, signed. I said, no, I'm not signing. I don't want to go through those operations and things like that. Because I said to myself, if I lived through till now what I lived through and I'm alive, I don't want to go through the operation. So I asked them about the operation. What is going to give me the benefit of the operation? They said they were going to put you together back straight. We're going to put in steel there. You know, 1945, they didn't have the knowledge yet. And so what are my chances of surviving? You're going to, to cut me. You're going to break me. You're going to cut me in the back. You're going to cut me all over. What is my chance of surviving? The doctor answered me, if everything goes well as scheduled, you got a 50%, 50%, 60% chance to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: The rest, we can't tell you anything else, just we're hoping that everything is going to go well-- about 60%. How long do I have to-- we're going to put you in, in [INAUDIBLE], you know like, the foot, the foot, the whole foot, and the whole body. And I have to lie there around about four months in a straight bed-- not to move, you know. Because they're gonna touch the back, you know, the spine. Oh, I said, everything is fine. Thank you very much for your kindness, what you want to do. You did that. You want to repay it. But I don't want no repayers. I want to go on as long as I can go on, I said, from bed now with crutches. And I can walk. And I can see somebody which we survived together. We can see each other. So I don't wanted no, no operation, no nothing. Fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they couldn't force me to anything. So they started to give me treatments, all kind of treatments. Like that time, they were calling it electric treatments-- like, you know, all kind of plates, you know, to my back and exercise and massages, things like that. It helped a little bit. But I started to come around. I started to walk already. I started-- I couldn't do things which I wanted to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, there was about-- in October sometimes, October, October or November, I don't recall exactly the time-- they released me from the hospital. Because no use to keep me in the hospital. The treatments, I could have come there every day. They picked me up. And they brought me back to the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: I found out-- somebody came back from Poland. And I found out that my brother, Carl, what I-- which I told you-- that he's alive and where he is and what. He's alive? It was a shock, you know? So then, I couldn't go to Poland as a Pole. So we were with another group. We had some guys, which they were, you know, going back and forth with all kind of merchandise, you know, like smuggling back and forth, you know? And they knew the road, the side roads, you know, to go over the, the border. So from Feldafing, I got in touch with those guys. And they said, sure, you can come with us. We're going to take you over the border from Germany to Czechoslovakia. And there in Czechlosovakia, you can go-- no problem to go to Poland. So I made an appointment with them and the time they went. And we went at night, when started to get dark. And I couldn't walk. So-- but they helped me. Somehow, we got over the border. We came to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the other side from Czechoslovakia, we took already transportation. And I came to Prague. And I registered in Prague that I am a Pole, born in Poland. They asked me from where I am born in Poland, in that, that city. And I am a Pole.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they gave me the r-- the right transportation to Poland in Czechoslovakia, in Prague, that time 1945, in October, Oct-- or no, in December, I think. It was in December. So they gave me papers, legal papers, that I am a Pole. And they gave me some-- a card that I can go on the train and go through the border. And I went to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the city which my brother was there. There, this city was Waldenburg. Waldenburg before the war, it was German. But when they, they, you know, they, they divided Poland, they took away [? Finland. ?] So they gave to Poland this part of Germany. So matter of fact, to this day, this is belongs to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came in December to Poland. And I found my brother. And we got together, you know. We were-- then in 19-- this was 1945 in December.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1946-- 1946-- the end of 1946, the beginning of 1947, my brother got married in Poland in 1945, in December. And he stayed in December. We had, we had-- together with me, we e a store, a grocery store in Poland, in Waldenburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stay-- he stayed there because his wife got pregnant. So we stayed there till the end of-- the beginning of 1947. He stayed in Poland. And then he smuggled himself back to Germany, to Frankfurt, with his wife. Because she was pregnant, she didn't want to stay in Poland to have a baby. So they went back to Germany in 1947. And from Germany, my brother, which was here before the war, send them papers legally. They came to Canada, 1947.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remained in Poland till 1948, in April. And my brother send me also papers to Poland, legally. And I came from Poland to Canada. This was the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here in Canada, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to Canada, what could I do? I didn't have no profession of any kind. I was, when I came in 1948, I was 23 years old, also not healthy. Because I was suffering very badly. I went to all kinds of doctors, the best doctors, right away, the best doctors here in Canada. If you recall a doctor by the name [? Macnab, ?] you know, bone specialist. Shapiro, one of the best doctors here in Canada-- and, and they all told me the same thing. Send me for treatments. But if you want it, we can fix you up. We're going to break your leg. We're going to put you in this. We're going to put you-- and I said if I didn't do it that time, now it's a little bit later. And it's a little bit harder.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't. But I suffered. I suffered a lot. And right away, I-- my brother was here already. He came 1947. And he was a tailor. He learned the trade here. So right away, he took me to work in the same factory on Spadina. So I learned the trade as an operator in ladies' clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned the trade. It took me about maybe four weeks. I was the best there is in Spadina in four weeks' time. And I used to make good money, used to make money. But then in 1952, I decided to go in business. I wasn't married that, that time. I was single, still single. And I had a few dollars, because I make good money. I had a few dollars saved up. And that time, you didn't need too much money to go to business, as long you knew the trade. You open up a little two by four-- five machines, two machines, and a, and a pressing machine and things like that. So you in business. And that's the way we started. We were in business, together with my brother. And we were in business for 35 years together till we got separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is another story. In 1960-- I went in the business in 1952. 1960, I was already, like the saying in, in, in the [? feathers, ?] you know? I, I-- we, we had a factory already that time. And it was not too bad. It was good times there. And I was prosperous and made some money. And I was single, 1960. And I didn't go-- all those years which I was in business-- since I came 1948, I didn't go on no vacations, no nothing. Because the whole thing was revolved around work-- going to in the morning, coming home 12 o'clock, seven days a week or six days a week. Was nothing else but work, work, work, work, make $1. That's all. 1960, I decided to go to Israel. So my brother says, you, you, you never go away no place. He used to go already, you know, with his wife. He had a, a kid, you know, one daughter, and things like that. So he used to go. So you know, he was anxious that I should go also someplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to myself, why can't I go? I'm by myself. Why can't I go? But I heard in from Israel that they have a lot of landsmen there, you know, from this same city. So I figured 1960, I go to Israel. So I went to Israel. In, in, May, 1960 I went to Israel. I was in Israel 10 weeks. And all the landsmen which I was with-- and they didn't see me for a long time. They knew more then. So happened in, in, in my place here in Spadina was working a guy, a shipper, the main shipper, a Jewish guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had a brother in Israel. So he gave me the address with the phone number. He says, the first thing when you come there, you go to my brother. And he is going to take you. He is going to-- you can sleep with-- in his place. But I made a reservation in a hotel. And I went into hotel. Then my landsman came. And he didn't let me stay in the hotel. He took me out to his house. You know, it was like a, a reunion with something or so many. Being in Israel about three, four weeks-- four weeks I think. So somebody-- everybody was anxious, you know, to introduce me-- oh, this girl, and this girl, this girl, and this girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, OK, I didn't come here to get married. What do you want me to do? I-- fine, you want to introduce me. So finally, I met my wife through introductions, you know. She was working in a store in Israel, in a delicatessen store-- like, you know, just, just fruits and cakes and coffee and things like that, like a cafeteria, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met there. And we went out together a couple of times. And right after-- you know, you're here for such a short time. And I had already plans to go home. I supposed to be away five weeks. And this was already four weeks. Another week, I can't get. But she met me. And, and we started to go out. We went out a couple of times. She didn't let me go. No way you gonna go. You marry me. That's what she says. That's what she said.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, listen, I'm going home. I'm not here-- no, you stay. So you know, we got together. And we went out a few times, another one. And she dragged me. You know, she was speaking Hebrew. I didn't know no Hebrew. I was talking to her Polish. She didn't even know Jewish, how to speak. I was talking to her Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: We decided, I decided-- I was writing to my brother that I can't come home in this time. I have to postpone my tickets. And something is happening which I am going to let you know what's going on. And I stayed another two weeks. We went out practically every day, every night. She made arrangement for our wedding, for everything. And I was thinking like a fool, you know. [LAUGHS] Let, let myself talk into. And that's why we got married in July, 1960.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking back throughout your time, throughout your Holocaust years, can you tell your children and grandchildren and future generations, what your feelings are, what your hopes, desires? Can you share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they know the stories from inside out. My daughter knows, because she was working here with the Holocaust. And she was involved with the, with the-- because I told her this story. She heard those stories a lot of times. And what, what can I tell them more that to remember what happened. That they should have it in their-- you know, it shouldn't be able to happen again. That's all. That's all that I can tell them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that feisty little boy, what happened to that feisty boy? You, as a feisty boy, now that you're an adult, are you still that fighter?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I-- now, mine, mine life is family, you know? I got the one daughter, grandchildren. They should be able to grow up and have a little bit of [INAUDIBLE]. And we should strive, you know, to a better world. And we shouldn't, you know, think about it that it can never, never happen again. And that's what-- To be politically involved or something like that, I'm not.

speaker: interviewer
text: But about your belief systems?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the sister?

speaker: interviewer
text: The belief systems, in God, in Judaism.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I believe in God. But like I had-- mine, mine daughter is religious, very religious. My son-in-law is a doctor, a specialist, a cardiologist. And he's a [INAUDIBLE]-- you know, very religious. So I had the conversations with him, with my daughter. I believe only, like the saying, 98% that there's nothing, such a thing like God or-- you know, like, I don't believe in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why 98% you will ask? Because in case I'm wrong, 2% is good enough to have, you know, that was wrong. 98%, 2%, you're OK. If would be there something, like God or things like that-- could never, should of never happened, a thing like that. Because I have-- you know, I have proof that in Poland, there was Jews, like in our city. They didn't-- their whole life was nothing else but God in religion, learning, go to shul, in, in, in all those. You know, like a sin, was with them-- not such a thing like a sin. And it could happen, a thing like that-- burning synagogues, which this was our holy place, synagogues, taking live people, putting in the synagogue, burn them alive. Jews which I have seen myself cut with knives, their beards, which they never, never they sin in their life. So how can you believe it? If there's such a thing like miracles, like the Bible says-- our Bible is full of miracles, starting with Moses. with the whole generations all around, nothing else but miracles. Why could not happen one miracle to the Germans that they would have a lesson not to do a thing like that? Why? That explanation, I am asking my son-in-law all the time. He said that not of that, we wouldn't have Israel. We wouldn't have this. This is written the Bible. That's way it's supposed to-- that's the way it's supposed to be, that we are the only nation in the world to suffer? Why? Did we do so much harm to anybody? They do it to us all the time. And it's still going on. How long, who knows? So that's what I was-- my only hope and prayer is that it should never happen again, that's all. Do you have anything else to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing else, you know, particular-- I'll, I can add to my story, you know, that my life and mine whole surviving is a miracle. This I believe, that not so far a miracle, but this is like a destiny. That mine destiny, you know, like a [? goral. ?] You know what I mean in Jewish, [INAUDIBLE]-- a destiny-- that this probably is supposed to be like that, whether it's from God or whether it came from, from nowhere, my luck or something like that. I can't tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm trying to figure it out which way, whether this-- but I am, I'm still believe in, in Judaism, you know? I'm still going to, to synagogue, since I retired from work. Still go every Saturday to synagogue, every holiday, Jewish holiday, observe and things like that-- not observing 100%.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can drive on Saturday. I can drive on the holidays or something like that. It doesn't bother me. But I like to go to the Synagogue. I like to still keep on the Judaism going in the best way I can. But to believe 100% there's a God, no way. 98%, I don't believe-- 2%, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, thank you for that miracle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you're right. It's a miracle. This picture is when my, my father and my oldest brother, they were together in [? Shavnitza. ?] This was like a, a resort place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name-- my father is-- the first name is Abe, [INAUDIBLE] Avrum Abe. And my brother's name is Moishe [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this, about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- no, well, this was probably, who knows, 1930 or something. In my right, I can identify my mother, which sits in my right. Next to her--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Reisl. And next to her is mine sister-in-law with the two kids. One was Marmu. And one was Rifke.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister-in-law's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also Marmu. And then, on the bottom, sitting, is myself and my sister, Marmu. Then in the second row is my brother Motle, which he's still alive. He's here in Canada, and my father Avram Abe. This was his name. And then to my right again, in the top, was my brother, the oldest brother from the family, Moishe. Next to him is a brother also. His name is Haskel. He was-- he got shot in concentration camp in Skarzysko. Next to him is my sister, a sister. The name is Rivchu. And she got-- I think she went with the, the transport to Auschwitz. And then the last one in the side is also my sister, which she's still alive. She's about 85 years old, in Detroit. She lives in Detroit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bronia. Of what?

speaker: interviewer
text: History of the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This histor-- this picture was taken when my brother was-- got married in Stochov in 1935. After the wedding, we took a family picture. And this was a big thing at that time to take a family picture. Because you have to have-- you know, it wasn't sophisticated stuff like that. You know, today a picture like that, you know, there came a photographer with the whole machine. He covered himself. Watch me and do-- [LAUGHS] there was a whole commotion to make a picture like that. So this was it, 1935.
